title: SEGMENT 9 — What organoids can't do: vasculature, immune cells, maturity, size

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — What organoids can't do: vasculature, immune cells, maturity, size
Before we break for the hour mark, let me ground us in reality by enumerating what organoids cannot, today, do.
Because this is a field with a lot of justified excitement, and that excitement can sometimes spill over into overstatement.
Organoids are extraordinary research tools, but they are not whole organs in any meaningful sense, and there are a few specific limitations that constrain what they can be used for.
Limitation one: no vasculature.
This is the big one, and it's worth dwelling on.
In your body, every cell in every tissue is within about 100 to 200 microns of a blood vessel.
Beyond that distance, cells can't get enough oxygen and nutrients by diffusion, and they die.
Your body solves this by weaving capillaries throughout every tissue.
Organoids, growing in a dish, have no circulatory system.
They rely entirely on diffusion from the surrounding medium for oxygen, nutrients, and waste removal.

As the organoid grows, the cells in the center get further and further from the surface, and at some point — typically around 2 millimeters in diameter — the interior starts dying from lack of oxygen.
You get a necrotic core.
That sets a hard size ceiling on how big organoids can grow in a static dish.
Limitation two: no immune cells.
Organoids don't have the immune cell complement of real organs — no T cells, no B cells, usually no macrophages or microglia.
This matters for many applications.
You can't study the immune component of inflammatory disease in an organoid that lacks immune cells.
You can't model the interactions between cancer and the immune system if your tumor organoid is purely tumor cells.
You can't fully recapitulate infection biology if there are no immune cells responding to the pathogen.
Some labs are now adding immune cells back in — co-culturing organoids with T cells or microglia — but this is a recent development and it's far from perfect.
Limitation three: maturity.
Most organoids are developmentally immature.
They tend to resemble fetal or early-postnatal tissue more than adult tissue.
Cerebral organoids look like mid-trimester fetal brain.
Liver organoids often have fetal-like hepatocyte gene expression.

This is partly because organoid growth depends on stem and progenitor cells, which by their nature are less mature; and partly because the cells in vitro lack many of the maturation cues — hormonal, mechanical, neural — that drive postnatal tissue maturation in vivo.
The result is that organoids are better models of developing organs than of adult organs.
For studying adult-onset disease, this is a real limitation.
Limitation four: variability.
Different organoids from the same culture can look quite different from each other.
The unguided cerebral organoids from Lancaster's protocol, for example, have substantial organoid-to-organoid variability in what brain regions they contain and in what proportions.
Some labs have shown that the variability between organoids derived from a single iPSC line can rival the variability between iPSC lines from different donors.
This makes some experiments hard to interpret.
You need to use lots of organoids and apply careful statistical thinking.
The field is working on this — guided protocols, defined media, better quality control — but variability remains a challenge.
Limitation five: incomplete cellular composition.
Even within their core lineage, organoids often lack some of the cell types of the real organ.
Intestinal organoids often have weak Paneth cells.
Cerebral organoids have limited representation of certain neuronal subtypes.
Liver organoids often lack stellate cells and Kupffer cells.
Getting the full menagerie of cell types in proper proportions is an ongoing challenge.
The way to think about all of this is: organoids are partial models, not full replicas.
They capture a meaningful subset of real organ biology, and the subset they capture is enormously useful, but they don't capture everything.
The mistake to avoid is treating an organoid as if it were the same thing as a real organ.

A drug that works on a kidney organoid might fail in a patient because the organoid lacked the immune component that drives drug clearance, or because the organoid was developmentally immature and didn't have the adult kidney's full metabolic profile.
A neural circuit you observe in a cerebral organoid might not correspond to a circuit in a real brain because the organoid lacks the long-range connectivity of an intact nervous system.
The field is acutely aware of these limitations and is actively working on each of them.
Organoid-on-chip approaches — which we'll talk about in detail in Segment 11 — try to address the vasculature problem with microfluidic perfusion.
Co-culture approaches try to address the missing-cell-type problem by adding back immune cells or endothelial cells or stromal cells.
Maturation approaches try to extend culture times and add maturation cues to push organoids closer to adult identity.
Assembloid approaches — Segment 14 — fuse organoids of multiple types to recapitulate the connections between organ systems.
But for any application, you should always ask: what does this organoid model and what does it not model?
And does the thing I care about depend on what's modeled, or on what's missing?
If you're asking about epithelial cell biology, missing immune cells may not matter.
If you're asking about inflammatory disease, it matters enormously.
Match the model to the question.
That's the appropriate place to take a breath and stop for our break.
